title: Autoregressive Next-Token Prediction
concept: LLMs are pretrained by predicting the next token in a sequence: given a prompt, predict the probability distribution over the vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: What type of AI models rely on predicting the next token in a sequence to generate text?
exercises.explain: Autoregressive next-token prediction is the foundation of modern Large Language Models (LLMs), allowing them to generate fluent text.

cards.title: The Magic Behind the Screen
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how ChatGPT writes out its answers one word at a time, almost like it's thinking in real-time? It's not magic—it's a super-charged game of guessing the very next word over and over again!

cards.title: What is Next-Token Prediction?
cards.body: Next-Token Prediction is the core training method for LLMs. Given a sequence of words (a prompt), the AI looks at its entire vocabulary and calculates a probability score for every single word to decide which one should come next. The term 'autoregressive' means it uses its own previous outputs to make the next guess.

cards.title: The Word-Guessing Game
cards.body: Imagine you are playing a game of 'fill in the blank' with a friend. If they say: 'The cat sat on the...', your brain instantly highlights words like 'mat' or 'sofa' instead of 'banana' or 'swims'. An LLM does the exact same thing! It looks at what has been written so far and guesses the most likely next word from its mental dictionary.

cards.title: Probability in Action
cards.body:
If the input prompt is 'Spaghetti is a type of...', the model calculates probabilities for the next word. It might assign:
• 'pasta': 85%
• 'food': 12%
• 'car': 0.001%
Because 'pasta' has the highest probability, the model is highly likely to choose it next!

cards.title: Do LLMs Plan Ahead?
cards.body: Many people think AI models plan out their entire paragraphs and sentences before they start writing. In reality, they are just predicting one token at a time based on what they've already written. They live entirely in the moment, one word at a time!

cards.title: Human Brain vs. AI Predictor
cards.body: Let's compare how we write human thoughts versus how next-token prediction builds text.

left.text: We form a complete idea or point in our minds first, then translate it into structured sentences.

right.text: Calculates the mathematical probability of the next word based only on the words that came before it.

exercises.prompt: How are Large Language Models (LLMs) pretrained on large datasets?

options.text: By translating every sentence into binary math formulas.
By memorizing dictionary definitions of all words.
By predicting the next token in a sequence over and over.
By asking humans for feedback on every single word.

exercises.explain: During pretraining, LLMs learn by reading massive amounts of text and trying to predict the very next word in those texts.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In autoregressive prediction, the words the AI generates are fed back into the prompt to help it predict the next word.
exercises.explain: Autoregressive generation means the model uses its own previous predictions as input context to predict the next words.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how an LLM evaluates potential next words.
exercises.sentence: To pick the next token, the AI calculates a score called the vocabulary distribution.
exercises.explain: When predicting the next token, the LLM assigns a probability score to every word in its vocabulary to see which one is the most likely fit.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents next-token prediction in action?

options.text: A programmer writing a Python function to print 'blue'.
A user asking 'What color is the sky?'
An AI completing 'The sky is...' with 'blue' based on word statistics.

exercises.explain: The AI output (Choice C) is generated word-by-word based on statistical patterns of what typically follows 'The sky is'. Choices A and B show humans making structural decisions or asking questions rather than predicting the next token.

exercises.prompt: How does a Large Language Model generate a long, multi-word response using next-token prediction?

options.text: It generates the entire paragraph all at once in a single parallel step.
It predicts one word, adds it to the prompt, and uses that new prompt to predict the next word.
It guesses the middle word first, then works its way outward to the edges.
It looks at the very last word only and ignores all previous context.

exercises.explain: Autoregressive next-token prediction works step-by-step. The AI predicts one token, adds it to the prompt, and then uses this updated, longer prompt to predict the very next token. This loop repeats to build entire sentences.
